slides.title: Day 11 — From Financial Literacy to Financial Entrepreneurship
slides.body:
Week 3: Discover a business model.

You are not here to learn what financial advisors do.
You are here to discover why financial advice is one of the best businesses you can build.
slides.speakerNotes:
Opening — don't read the slide.
Walk toward the audience. Pause.
Say: Now we're talking.
Tell the three-week story slowly:
Week 1 — you discovered opportunities.
Week 2 — you proved the opportunity exists.
Week 3 — now you need a business that creates value.
This is the shift from financial literacy to financial entrepreneurship.
discussionQuestions: What changed between Week 1 and Week 2 in how you see your venture?

slides.title: Week 1 — You Discovered Opportunities
slides.speakerNotes:
Callback to Week 1.
Ask the room: What problem did your squad discover?
Who is your customer?
What solution did you imagine?
Do not rush — let them feel the continuity.
discussionQuestions: Which Week 1 discovery still feels strongest after validation?

slides.title: Week 2 — You Proved the Opportunity Exists
slides.speakerNotes:
Callback to Week 2 validation.
Reference field interviews, FEC updates, Market Validation Pitch.
The market spoke. The opportunity is real.
Now the entrepreneur question: what business will you build on it?
discussionQuestions: What evidence from Week 2 surprised you most?

slides.title: Now You Need a Business That Creates Value
slides.body:
Discovery and validation are not enough.
Entrepreneurs build ventures.
slides.speakerNotes:
Transition to the central question of Week 3.
Speak SPIKE language: venture builders don't stop at research — they design a business model.

slides.title: The Zero-Capital Question
slides.body:
What business can you start today…
with almost zero capital…
that creates real impact…
while building recurring income?
slides.speakerNotes:
Pause. Let silence work.
Do not answer yet.
Let participants think.
Write their guesses on the board if available.
discussionQuestions: What businesses did you think of?
Which require capital you don't have?

slides.title: Reveal — A Professional Financial Advisory Practice
slides.body:
Not because it's AIA.
Because it's the venture.
slides.speakerNotes:
Reveal slowly.
A Professional Financial Advisory Practice.
Now AIA becomes relevant — not as an employer, but as the venture you can build.
This is the mind shift.
discussionQuestions: Did anyone in the room think of this before the reveal?

slides.title: Why Do Millions Struggle Financially?
slides.speakerNotes:
Opportunity Analysis begins here — not with a financial picture of the client.
Ask: Why do millions of Filipinos struggle financially despite working hard?
Collect answers. Validate with empathy, not judgment.
discussionQuestions: Why do hardworking people still struggle financially?

slides.title: Who Helps Solve This Problem?
slides.body:
Teacher? Bank? Government?
Insurance Company? Financial Planner? Financial Advisor?
slides.speakerNotes:
Poll the room on each option.
Discuss what each can and cannot do.
Guide toward: there is a gap that a trusted advisor can fill.
discussionQuestions: Who do families actually trust with money decisions?

slides.title: The Entrepreneur Lens
slides.body:
There is a market.
There is a problem.
There is demand.
There is impact.
There is income.
slides.speakerNotes:
That is exactly how entrepreneurs think.
Connect each line to their Week 1–2 work.
Market = segments they interviewed.
Problem = validated pain.
Demand = willingness to pay signals.
Impact = families becoming financially secure.
Income = recurring advisory relationships.
discussionQuestions: Which of these five did you already prove in Week 2?

slides.title: Could YOUR Venture Be YOU?
slides.body:
Could your business model be:
Helping families become financially secure?
slides.speakerNotes:
This is the mind-blowing shift.
Pause after asking.
Let it land.
You are not joining a company.
You are building a venture — and the venture might be you as a professional financial advisor.
discussionQuestions: What would that mean for your ambition statement?

slides.title: AIA — Platform, Not Employer
slides.speakerNotes:
Introduce AIA as a platform — not an employer.
Contrast: employee mindset vs venture builder mindset.
You bring professional skills. AIA provides the platform. You serve clients. You earn income. You build a business. You grow into leadership. You build an enterprise.
discussionQuestions: What's the difference between joining a company and building on a platform?

slides.title: The Platform Stack
slides.body:
YOU
↓ Professional Skills
↓ AIA Platform
↓ Clients
↓ Income
↓ Business
↓ Leadership
↓ Enterprise
slides.speakerNotes:
Draw this on the board if possible.
Walk down each layer slowly.
Let them see the full venture architecture.
This is SPIKE — startup thinking applied to financial advice.

slides.title: Deck 01 Close — Opportunity Analysis Workshop
slides.body: Afternoon: map the opportunity. Upload to Venture Portfolio.
slides.speakerNotes:
Transition to Deck 02 workshop.
Preview tomorrow: Your First Business Tool — the FNA.
